questions.text: In a right-angled triangle, which side is the hypotenuse?
choices: The side adjacent to the chosen angle $\theta$
The side opposite the chosen angle $\theta$
The longest side, opposite the right angle
Whichever side is drawn at the bottom
questions.explanation: The hypotenuse is always the longest side and is always opposite the $90^\circ$ angle — it doesn't depend on which other angle you're using, unlike the opposite and adjacent sides.
SOH = Sin is Opposite over Hypotenuse, so $\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.

questions.text: Which of the following are correct trig ratio definitions for a right-angled triangle, relative to angle $\theta$?
questions.explanation: SOHCAHTOA gives $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}}$ and $\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$. The fourth statement has tan upside down, so it is incorrect.

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has two shorter sides of length $6\,\text{cm}$ and $8\,\text{cm}$. Use Pythagoras' theorem to find the hypotenuse.
A right-angled triangle has hypotenuse $13\,\text{cm}$ and one shorter side $5\,\text{cm}$. Find the other shorter side.
A right-angled triangle has hypotenuse $7\,\text{cm}$ and an angle of $35^\circ$. Find the side opposite the $35^\circ$ angle, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Opposite and hypotenuse are involved, so use SOH: $\sin 35^\circ = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{7}$, so $\text{opposite} = 7 \times \sin 35^\circ \approx 7 \times 0.5736 \approx 4.0\,\text{cm}$.

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has hypotenuse $12\,\text{cm}$ and an angle of $52^\circ$. Find the side adjacent to the $52^\circ$ angle, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Adjacent and hypotenuse are involved, so use CAH: $\cos 52^\circ = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{12}$, so $\text{adjacent} = 12 \times \cos 52^\circ \approx 12 \times 0.6157 \approx 7.4\,\text{cm}$.

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has an adjacent side of $9\,\text{cm}$ next to an angle of $40^\circ$. Find the hypotenuse, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Adjacent and hypotenuse are involved, so use CAH: $\cos 40^\circ = \dfrac{9}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, so $\text{hypotenuse} = \dfrac{9}{\cos 40^\circ} \approx \dfrac{9}{0.7660} \approx 11.7\,\text{cm}$.

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has an adjacent side of $15\,\text{cm}$ next to an angle of $28^\circ$ (no hypotenuse given). Find the opposite side, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: With no hypotenuse involved, use TOA: $\tan 28^\circ = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{15}$, so $\text{opposite} = 15 \times \tan 28^\circ \approx 15 \times 0.5317 \approx 8.0\,\text{cm}$.

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has opposite side $5\,\text{cm}$ and hypotenuse $9\,\text{cm}$. Find the angle opposite the $5\,\text{cm}$ side, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Opposite and hypotenuse are known, so use SOH: $\sin\theta = \dfrac{5}{9} \approx 0.5556$, so $\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.5556) \approx 33.7^\circ$.

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has adjacent side $8\,\text{cm}$ and hypotenuse $11\,\text{cm}$. Find the angle between them, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Adjacent and hypotenuse are known, so use CAH: $\cos\theta = \dfrac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273$, so $\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.7273) \approx 43.3^\circ$.

questions.text: You know the opposite and adjacent sides of a right-angled triangle, but not the hypotenuse, and need a missing angle. Which trig ratio (sin, cos or tan) should you use?
questions.explanation: Opposite and adjacent, with no hypotenuse, is the TOA case: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$, so you use $\tan^{-1}$ to find the angle.
